findings.issue: Founder comprehension checkpoint: The 50,000+ word document lacks a plain-language executive summary explaining what the founder is paying for and what they should care about.
findings.evidence: D7 opens with "What D7 is not" (academic framing) rather than "What you're getting" (founder framing). After reading Section 1, Rafael would understand design philosophy but not why he should approve this vs. alternatives.
findings.recommendation: Add a 200-word "Founder's Guide to D7" before Section 1 that explains: (1) D7 is the complete spec for how Ocean Golf talks, looks, and behaves, (2) every button label and error message is specified so Phase 7 doesn't make guesses, (3) dark mode implementation is production-ready, (4) voice matches Rafael's personal style—approve or request changes now. Format as numbered bullet list, not paragraphs.

findings.issue: Toast message specifications lack clarity on what "color: Green" actually means—hex value is not stated, making it impossible for founders to verify or approve the visual appearance without opening a design tool.
findings.evidence: Example: "Toast: Success (API Response <500ms)" specifies "Color: Green (#10B981)" later in tables but inconsistently. In Section 2.1-2.4, color is sometimes stated as "Color: Green" without hex value; sometimes with hex.
findings.recommendation: Standardize all toast/message color specifications to include both name and hex value in the same line: "Color: Green (#10B981)" not "Green" or "Green (success semantic token)". This allows founder to verify the color palette matches their approval from Phase 6A without context-switching.

findings.issue: Form field validation specifications reference "soft" vs "hard" character limits without defining the business impact—founder cannot evaluate whether 500-char soft limit for notes is the right call.
findings.evidence: Section 3.4 states: "Character limit: 500 (soft; she can type up to 500 chars)" and later "No error message (character limit is soft; silently stops typing)". Appendix H (Design Debt) logs this as a debt item ("soft limit may exceed in practice"), but founder's input on this tradeoff is not captured.
findings.recommendation: Add a one-sentence business rationale to the soft limit design: "Character limits are soft (not hard) because Lucia is capable of self-regulating; hard limits feel punitive and slow her down. Trade-off: Notes might occasionally exceed 500 chars (logged in debt tracking; will be monitored in Phase 7 beta)." This lets founder decide if this tradeoff is acceptable or if hard limits are preferred.

findings.issue: Key concept 'Tier 2 Approval' is used throughout D7 but never clearly defined in non-technical language for founder understanding until Appendix F.
findings.evidence: Section 2.5 title reads "RESCHEDULE WITH COURSE CHANGE (Automatic Tier 2 Approval)" and Section 2.6 "REQUEST TIER 2 APPROVAL (Generic)"; both assume founder knows what Tier 2 means. Glossary definition is buried in Appendix F.
findings.recommendation: Add a one-sentence definition immediately after the first use of "Tier 2 Approval" in Section 2.5: "Tier 2 Approval: Escalations that require Rafael's explicit decision (course changes, price drops, group size changes outside your standard bounds). Lucia submits these, you review, and you approve or deny." This prevents founder confusion on first read.

findings.issue: Dark mode color specifications state hex values for dark palette but do not explicitly state which light-mode hex they replace, making it impossible for founder to verify the recalibration is correct without cross-referencing two sections.
findings.evidence: Section 4.2 states "Primary button | #0052A3 (navy) | #3B82F6 (light blue)" but does not explain why navy becomes light blue. Contrast table (Section 4.7) verifies the new pairing is WCAG AA compliant but does not justify the color choice to founder.
findings.recommendation: Add a one-sentence rationale column to the dark mode color mapping table: "Primary button | #0052A3 (navy) | #3B82F6 (light blue) | Navy invisible on dark background; light blue readable without glare for evening shifts." This explains the business reason for the change and helps founder evaluate if it's correct.

findings.issue: D7 Section 14 is a placeholder that redirects to D14 (parallel deliverable), but founder cannot see D14 in this review. This breaks self-containment—founder cannot evaluate accessibility sufficiency from D7 alone.
findings.evidence: Section 14 states "Separate Deliverable (D14)" and instructs reader to "See D14 Section X" multiple times. Without D14, founder cannot verify WCAG compliance, contrast ratios, or keyboard navigation are adequate.
findings.recommendation: Move D14 accessibility summary (color/contrast verification table, WCAG level confirmation, 44×44px touch target spec) into D7 Appendix as "Accessibility Checklist (Summary)" rather than placeholder. Keep full detail in D14, but give founder enough information in D7 to approve or reject accessibility direction. Alternatively, state explicitly: "D14 (Accessibility Compliance Spec) is generated in parallel and must be reviewed alongside D7 for complete approval."

findings.issue: Toast error messages specify technical error types (e.g., "Stripe monthly limit reached") but do not indicate what Lucia should actually do—founder cannot evaluate if error messaging provides sufficient guidance for field operators.
findings.evidence: Section 2.2 example: "Toast: Error — Stripe Monthly Limit Hit: Monthly Stripe limit reached ($50K) — Contact Rafael". The message identifies the problem but does not explain why this happened or what Lucia should tell you to fix it (e.g., "Increase limit from $50K to $75K").
findings.recommendation: Expand error message specs to include a brief note of what the operator should do next, even if the message itself is brief. Example: "Toast: 'Stripe limit reached ($50K) — Contact Rafael' [What Lucia will do: Tell Rafael 'We need to increase the monthly limit to $75K for this booking']." This shows founder that error messages result in productive escalation, not confused support requests.

findings.issue: Three workflow walkthroughs are detailed but do not include founder's expected behavior on the other side of approvals. Section 7.2 shows Lucia requesting approval but cuts off at "You tap it. Dashboard shows..."—founder doesn't see what his dashboard looks like or how he interacts with approvals.
findings.evidence: Section 7.2 demonstrates Lucia's approval submission but then pivots to "ON YOUR PHONE (Rafael)" with minimal context. Founder reads Lucia's 3-minute flow but not his own approval flow, making it hard to evaluate if approval system is reasonable from his perspective.
findings.recommendation: Expand Section 7.2 to include a parallel 'Rafael's Flow' section showing: (1) How approval notifications appear on his phone, (2) What information he sees on dashboard, (3) How he approves/denies, (4) Confirmation that Lucia receives decision in real-time. This gives founder confidence the two-sided flow is balanced and his approval load is reasonable.

findings.issue: Voice definition is abstract (4 adjectives: personal, confident, respectful, honest) without concrete examples of how these apply to real situations—founder cannot evaluate if this voice matches what he actually wants to communicate.
findings.evidence: Section 1.2 defines voice but does not provide side-by-side examples showing how the defined voice differs from alternatives. Example: "Personal, not corporate — 'Reminder sent' not 'Your reminder has been successfully submitted'" is provided, but only one example per principle.
findings.recommendation: Expand voice definition with 2-3 concrete examples per principle using actual error/success messages from D7 Section 2. Example under "Honest, not euphemistic": "'Stripe limit reached ($50K)' not 'Payment service unavailable'; this tells Lucia the specific problem so she can escalate effectively." This grounds abstract voice principles in real product language and helps founder confirm the tone is right.

findings.issue: Governance rules specify HOW developers should extend the system (use tokens, follow naming conventions) but do not address WHEN they should ask for founder approval vs. WHEN they can proceed independently—founder cannot evaluate if governance prevents scope creep or just adds process overhead.
findings.evidence: Section 6.1-6.4 specify reuse-vs-create framework, token discipline, naming conventions, and debt tracking, but do not define approval thresholds. Example: Is a new button variant (e.g., "button-secondary-disabled") an extension that needs founder review, or a standard pattern that developers can add freely?
findings.recommendation: Add a one-paragraph approval threshold definition: "New components or variants that change user-visible behavior require founder approval (coordinate with Phase 7 lead). Pure code refactoring (internal variable renaming, CSS restructuring) or variants that match existing patterns do not require approval. Design debt items logged in GitHub; reviewed weekly by Phase 7 lead; critical items escalated to founder." This prevents both decision paralysis and unilateral changes.

findings.issue: Decision records detail reasoning but do not indicate WHAT HAPPENS IF FOUNDER DISAGREES—this is critical for founder comprehension, because they need to know approval authority and timeline to revisit decisions if they change their mind.
findings.evidence: Decision D7-001 (Voice & Tone) includes reasoning but ends with "Reconsideration Condition: If user feedback indicates voice feels cold or disrespectful, revisit in Phase 8 with A/B testing." No mention of whether founder can revisit this decision NOW, or if voice is locked after Phase 6C approval.
findings.recommendation: Add a one-sentence owner/authority line to each decision record: "Owner: Rafael (founder). Locked until Phase 7 launch unless founder formally requests revision. Can be revisited in Phase 8 if Lucia provides operational feedback." This clarifies whether decisions are founder-approved and locked, or provisional pending Phase 7 validation.

findings.issue: D7 does not address the gap between what's specified here (100+ phrases, dark mode, interactions) and what's actually MISSING that Phase 7 still has to design—founder may believe D7 specifies the entire UI, not realizing large areas (search, analytics, admin UI) are deferred.
findings.evidence: D7 explicitly states "Phase 7 implements toast notifications only (Channel 1). Push notifications (Channel 2) and email digests (Channel 3) deferred to Phase 8+" in Section 2.11, but this is buried. No summary section explains the scope boundaries.
findings.recommendation: Add a "What D7 Does NOT Cover" callout box early (after Executive Summary): "D7 is complete for MVP (hunt list, detail panel, core actions). Deferred to Phase 8+: search, analytics dashboard, push notifications, email digests, multi-user presence, drag-and-drop reordering, illustrated empty states, white-labeling. Phase 7 implements only text-based empty states and MVP core features." This prevents founder from assuming D7 covers everything.

findings.issue: Design decision ledger references "D-55" numbering but founder cannot easily find the actual decision details without hunting through Section 15—ledger should point to the full decision record location.
findings.evidence: Appendix B lists "D55-P6C-001 Voice & Tone — Personal Over Corporate [✅ Approved]" but does not indicate where the full decision record lives. Founder would need to search Section 15 to find "Decision D7-001" (different numbering).
findings.recommendation: Update Appendix B table to include a "Full Details" column: "| D55-P6C-001 | Section 15 (Decision D7-001) | Voice & Tone — Personal Over Corporate | ✅ Approved |". This allows founder to quickly jump to detailed reasoning if they want to revisit a decision.

findings.issue: Icon library selection (Heroicons) is justified by research findings but founder cannot see the research summary in the main text—they must jump to Section 17 or Appendix, making it hard to evaluate if icon library choice is solid.
findings.evidence: Section 5.1 states "Library verified active maintenance as of March 2026" but does not show the research findings. Research Point 6C-001 summarizes findings in Section 17, but founder reviewing Section 5 would not know to look there.
findings.recommendation: Add a 2-3 sentence research summary directly in Section 5.1: "Heroicons verified via Perplexity research (Feb 2026 release, 312 icons, MIT license, actively maintained by Tailwind Labs, 95%+ developer sentiment). No maintenance risk identified through 2027. See Section 17 for full research details." This keeps founder in one place and provides instant confidence in the selection.

findings.issue: Microcopy specs are comprehensive but vary in format/completeness—some sections (e.g., 2.1 Send Reminder) include button state, loading, multiple error scenarios, and success; others (e.g., 2.8 Create Booking) focus on modal form without post-submission detail. Founder may wonder if some interactions are underspecified.
findings.evidence: Section 2.1 includes: button label, loading state, success toast, error toast (network), error toast (WhatsApp service), error toast (invalid phone), detail panel after success, detail panel after error + retry, detail panel after multiple failures. Section 2.8 ends after [CREATE BOOKING] button is tapped; post-creation behavior is mentioned briefly ("Hunt list item updates") but not as detailed as Section 2.1's post-send specifications.
findings.recommendation: Standardize the level of detail across all major actions. For each action, specify: (1) button label/state, (2) loading state if applicable, (3) success scenario with toast/page update, (4) error scenarios with recovery paths, (5) post-action detail panel/hunt list state. This ensures founder knows every interaction is equally well-specified, or identifies areas that need expansion before Phase 7 starts.

findings.issue: Legal/consent routes are marked "out of scope for Phase 7" but founder may not realize this means no login terms, no privacy policy route, no cookie banner will exist in MVP—this could create surprise when Phase 7 launch is ready.
findings.evidence: Section 12B states "Ocean Golf MVP (Phase 7) requires minimal legal/consent flows" and later "No legal/consent flows in Phase 7 scope", but does not explicitly state what this means for launch (e.g., "You'll need to provide privacy/terms URLs before launch; if not ready, Phase 7 team will add placeholder links pointing to external docs").
findings.recommendation: Add a one-sentence clarification: "Phase 7 will not include cookie consent, privacy policy routes, or terms acceptance flows; these are deferred to Phase 8. Before launch, provide links to your privacy policy and terms of service for footer links (if footer added in Phase 8) or contact form. No legal blocking for Phase 7 launch." This prevents last-minute surprises around legal obligations.

findings.issue: Theming architecture is documented as "future-proofing for Phase 8+" but founder may not understand the implication—if theming is baked into design tokens now, what does that cost in Phase 7 complexity?
findings.evidence: Appendix D explains themeable vs. fixed tokens and future implementation strategy, but does not state whether design token architecture must be finalized now to support Phase 8 theming, or if it can be added later without refactoring.
findings.recommendation: Add a one-sentence clarification: "Design tokens are structured to support future white-labeling (Phase 8+) without additional Phase 7 refactoring. No extra work required in Phase 7; Phase 8 simply loads custom theme values. If white-labeling is abandoned, token structure remains unchanged." This assures founder that future-proofing does not add Phase 7 cost.
